text:
Person1: I ain't never used no hatchet that I remember.
Person2: You're just crazy in a retarded kind of way then.  It wouldn't matter to me if you did do violence on somebody 'cause I ain't afraid of shit. You think I'm afraid for you to stay here. You're just a bit odd it looks like to me. Not really, I'm just jokin' with you. Welcome to our humble home, buddy. Frank needs all the friends he can get. Frank's a real weak little kid. His daddy taught him how to be a timid person.

Person1: How long have you been in Alaska?
Person2: I don't remember.  Is that what you wanted to ask me?

Person1: Take it easy!  I didn't have anything to do with him getting killed if that's what you mean.
Person2: But you were business associates?
Person1: What does that have to do --

Person1: There must be some other way out.
Person2: Well, until that's found, I think it's a good idea we all stick together.  Or wouldn't that fit into your plans, baby?

Person1: I have a proposal, Mr. Kay.  This mode of questioning is getting old, don't you think?
Person2: I don't mind.
Person1: Let's make it more interesting.  I will answer any question you ask. But, for each answer, you must remove one article of clothing.

Person1: He is a very close friend.  Do you have some explanation of what happened in my apartment?
Person2: Yes, but I have to tell you in private at a fine restaurant.
Person1: Can't you tell me now?
Person2: I'll cancel the reservation,  I found the name "Zuul" in ...  The Roylance Guide to Secret Societies and Sects.  I don't suppose you've  read it.
Person1: You must have gotten the last copy.
Person2: Well, the name Zuul refers to a demi-god worshipped around 6000 B.C. by the ... What's that say?
Person1: Hittites,  the Mesopotamians and the Sumerians.  "Zuul was the Minion of Gozer."
Person2: "Gozer" -- he was very big in the Sumerian religion.  One of their gods.
Person1: What's he doing in my refrigerator.
Person2: I'm checking on that.  I think we should meet Thursday night at nine to talk about it.
Person1: I don't think so.  I'm busy Thursday night.
Person2: You think I enjoy giving up my evenings to spend time with clients?  I'm making an exception because I respect you as an artist and as a dresser.
Person1: All right.  Since you put it that way.
Person2: I'll pick you up at your place.  I'll bring along the Roylance Guide -- we can read after we eat.
Person1: I've got to go now.

Person1: Get up out of the chair, Richard.
Person2: What's with you people? Why do ya have to mess with my head all the time? I came through, man.
Person1: Get up out of the chair, and walk toward the backdoor, Richard.
Person2: I get thrown in solitary until I don't know my own darn name, and then you people tell me I can beat nine years if I help you. I helped you!
Person1: Get up!

Person1: So where do you know Alan from?
Person2: We play tennis at the club.
Person1: Really. The California Racquet Club?
Person2: Yes.
Person1: That's my club too. I haven't seen you there.
Person2: Well, I haven't played in a while because of these kidney pains.
Person1: Right, and how long have you had these pains, Mr. Barber?
Person2: That's Babar.
Person1: Two bs?
Person2: One. B-a-b-a-r.
Person1: That's two.
Person2: But not right next to each other. I thought that's what you meant.
Person1: Arnold Babar. Isn't there a children's book about an elephant named Babar?
Person2: I don't know. I don't have any.
Person1: No children?
Person2: No books. No elephants either. No really good elephant books.
Person1: Still, it'd an odd name. I don't remember seeing it on the club registry.

Person1: Don't get all worked up, Justin. I expected some common sense on your part and clearly I was expecting too much. Just open the drawer.
Person2: We never should have done this to him...
Person1: Well, what are we gonna do about it now?

Person1: All right... where did you see her?
Person2: She's in my biology class.
Person1: Did you get her number?
Person2: No.
Person1: Did you get her name?
Person2: No. It's too soon.
Person1: It's never too soon! Girls decide how far to let you go in the first five minutes.
Person2: Well, what do you want me to do? Go up to this strange girl in my biology class and say, 'Hello! I'd like you to take your clothes off and jump on me?'
Person1: I would. Yeah.
Person2: Really?
Person1: I can see it all now. This is going to be just like the girl you fell in love with at Fotomat this summer. You bought forty bucks of film and you never even talked to her.
Person2: You tell me, Mike. What do I do?
Person1: Okay. Okay.  Here's what you do.

Person1: Dom? What are you?
Person2: You stole her from me. Now I want her back.

Person1: What will you do now?
Person2: You needn't worry Miss Cartwright. I've been at this a very long time.
Person1: He called you "MacLeod".
Person2: Not your concern.
Person1: I left a man dead in Felton.  But you don't really care, do you?
Person2: That bothers you?
Person1: He was innocent.
Person2: He's dead.  Whatever I may or may not feel means exceedingly little to him now.
Person1: What about me?
Person2: You?
Person1: I'm a witness to a murder.  That seems to put me pretty high on your friend's chop list.
Person2: Have you gone to the police?
Person1: No.
Person2: Why not?  I'm sure they'd love to hear your story.
Person1: I'd rather hear yours.
Person2: You are being foolish.
Person1: I'm a historian, Mr. Taupin. Only once in a lifetime do you stare history in the face.
Person2: Go home.

Person1: Can you show me how this software works? I'm having a hard time figuring it out.
Person2: To be honest, Alex, it’s too complicated for you. You might be better off using something simpler.
Person1: I'm willing to learn, Riley. Just give me a chance.
Person2: I just don't want to waste time on something you might not grasp. But if you're that eager, I'll show you the basics.
Person1: That's all I'm asking for. Thanks for giving me a shot.

Person1: Don't tell me I don't have a choice!
Person2: Right.
Person1: I'm two seconds away from making my choice which means you've got two seconds to tell me why I shouldn't shoot you.
Person2: It's simple.  You need me.  I need you.  And we will both need money.
Person1: I don't need you to get the money -- my money!
Person2: If it hadn't been for me, you would be dead.

Person1: I'm gay. Like you didn't know.
Person2: Andrew. You're not gay; you're ten. You shouldn't even be having thoughts like...
Person1: You mean you didn't have any gay thoughts when you were my age...
Person2: Well, uh...Promise me you won't do anything until you're 18.
Person1: Did you wait until you were 18?

Person1: I'm so pleased you appreciate good wine. Have another glass!
Person2: Thank you, sir.
Person1: My wife... Alex freezes.
Person1: ... used to do everything for me and leave me to my writing.
Person2: Your wife, sir? Has she gone away?
Person1: No. She's dead!
Person2: I'm sorry to hear about that, sir. His face contorted in rage.
Person1: She was very badly raped, you see. We were assaulted by a gang of vicious young hooligans in this house, in this very room you're sitting in now. I was left a helpless cripple. The doctors said it was Pneumonia, because it happened some months later during the 'flu epidemic. The doctors told me it was Pneumonia, but I knew what it was. A victim of the modern age, poor, poor girl. Suddenly his mood changes. He wheels right up to Alex.
Person1: And now you, another victim of the modern age. But you can be helped. I phoned some friends while you were having a bath.
Person2: Phoned some friends, sir?
Person1: Yes. They want to help.
Person2: Help me, sir?
Person1: Help you.
Person2: Who are they, sir?
Person1: They're very, very important people and they're interested in you. Bell rings. Julian rises,
Person1: Julian. This will be these people now. Alex gets up.
Person2: Look, sir. I'm sorry to have troubled you. I think I ought to be going, sir. Julian bars the way.
Person1: No, no my boy. No trouble at all. Alex slowly sits.

Person1: Big giant capacitor.  And that, instead of generating power it'll sort of be --  -- sucking power, from Gotham City, and storing it ... stockpiling it, sort of?  Which, unless I'm being dense, is a novel approach, I'd say.
Person2: And who ... would you say this to?

Person1: I didn't say that.  Why would I say that?
Person2: Well Daddy and I were thinking that sometimes people tell a story about what they wish would happen.
Person1: I don't want that to happen.  Why would I want that to happen?
Person2: Well you're telling your teachers and your friends --
Person1: Mass hysteria.
Person2: Maybe you're upset that Rachel moved in.
Person1: I'm not upset.  Why would I be upset?
Person2: Look if the truth is you don't feel like talking about this right now that's fine. But don't look me in the eye with a big smile on your face and lie to me. Cause there are only so many lies you're allowed to tell before it starts showing on your face.  You wind up looking like...

Person1: Wants to fix up her makeup, no doubt.
Person2: Is Looseleaf Harper alive?
Person1: Alive and hale.  He's throwing a little surprise party for his own family.  Is your mother often this unstable?  Penelope!
Person2: She's a real heavy sleeper sometimes.
Person1: Why don't you go to bed--son.
Person2: I can't take my eyes off you.
Person1: Tomorrow's another day. PAUL You know what my English literature teacher said about you?
Person1: Can't it keep till morning?
Person2: She said you were legendary.  I wrote a theme about you, and she said, "Your father is a legendary hero out of the Golden Age of Heroes."
Person1: That's nice.  You thank her for me. Go to bed and get lots of sleep, and then you thank her in the morning.
Person2: Tomorrow's Saturday.  Anyway, she's dead.
Person1: Penelope!
Person2: She was killed in the park two months ago--in the daytime.
Person1: Penelope!
Person2: She was on her way home from a meeting of the African Violet Society, and they got her.
Person1: Will you go to bed?
Person2: Yes sir.  If you can't wake Mom up, I've got double-decker bunks.
Person1: Scat!

Person1: Where did you get this cauliflower?
Person2: From your office, sir.
Person1: Didn't you prepare it with the celery before my lecture?
Person2: Yes, sir.  But I must have taken the wrong one when we came up.
Person1: Are you trying to make me look like an idiot?

Person1: Alone again.  What are you up to today?
Person2: I'm doing some volunteer work.
Person1: Need any company?
Person2: You? Volunteer? I don't think so.
Person1: I don't know? Maybe I'd like it. I'm trying to change here. You could be supportive.
Person2: Okay.
Person1: Babe, you're looking at the next Mother Teresa.

Person1: What do you want, Senator?
Person2: Saunders--it's going to go pretty bad for Jeff tomorrow. There's only one thing that can be done for him now--  I--I've written his resignation. He resigns under protest--denying all charges. No one will ever be sure if he was guilty or not. It leaves him with at least a shred of honor. The other way--branded openly in the Senate--expelled--he'll never live it down. Rather a simple compromise than utter ruin. In a year--the whole thing might be forgotten--
Person1: What are you driving at? You want me to get him to sign that?
Person2: Yes--
Person1: Why don't you do it yourself?
Person2: He's lost complete faith in me--
Person1: Well--me, too!
Person2: But--you love him, don't you, Saunders?
Person1: What are you talking about? What difference--?
Person2: Do you?
Person1: All right--yes! And what does that make me to him? Nothing! I've got to go about my own business--and forget it!
Person2: I thought I could, too.  My business--this fine future! I have no future I care about, if this boy is broken! I--I can't sleep. The only important thing in my life now is to save what I can for him. I want him to get a start again--I'll see that he's taken care of as long as he lives--!  Saunders--whether you ever mean anything to him or not--
Person1: Me! Me ! I still don't see why I should--! If you love him so much, why don't you go to him yourself and-- ? Or better still--get up in that Senate and fight for him!
Person2: It's too late now--it's impossible!
Person1: So I go right back where I was-- carrying compromises--covering up-- back to political tricks--this time for--! No! I was just getting rid of all that. If I did anything, I ought to go and tell him to stand up and--. No! I don't want any part of it! Smith or anything else! I'm all through. I want to be left alone!

Person1: You are?
Person2: Yes, I am.
Person1: What are you trying to tell me?
Person2: What Roma's trying to tell you. What I told you yesterday.  Why you don't belong in this business.
Person1: Why I don't...
Person2: You listen to me, someday you might say, "Hey..." No, forget that, you just listen what I'm going to say: your partner depends on you.  Your partner...a man who's your "partner" depends on you...you have to go with him and for him...or you're shit, you're shit, you can't exist alone...
Person1: Excuse me...
Person2: ...excuse me, nothing, you be as cold as you want, but you just messed up a good man out of six thousand dollars and his goddamn bonus 'cause you didn't know the shot, if you can do that and you aren't man enough that it gets you, then I don't know what, if you can't take some thing from that...  you're scum, you're fucking white-bread.  You be as cold as you want. A child would know it, he's right.  You're going to make something up, be sure it will help or keep your mouth closed.

Person1: He didn't have to hit him. It's not exactly like I'm sleepin' with the guy! And he makes me sneak around to see my own friends! What is that all about?
Person2: Well, I guess it's 'cause he loves you so much. He's jealous and worried.
Person1: He cares about what I do?
Person2: Look, I'll try to find out what the hell's goin' on. When I see him I'll talk to him.
Person1: Okay.
Person2: All right?
Person1: Yeah.  Thanks.

Person1: Are you Klingman?
Person2: Who's asking?
Person1: Where can we talk?
Person2: Right here.
Person1: I represent the interests of the Corleone family.  We make the invitation to you to tie up your affairs and be out of the hotel by Monday morning.
Person2: Who do you think you're talking to?
Person1: You said you were Klingman.
Person2: You don't come in here, talk to an owner in Las Vegas like that.
Person1: You missed my point; you are no longer an owner.
Person2: Get out of my hotel.

Person1: Get a dance yet?
Person2: Nope.
Person1: Me neither.
Person2: Who'd you ask?
Person1: Tina Wilcox.
Person2: You're crazy.  She's Ed's girl friend.
Person1: Doesn't hurt to ask.  Sometimes the most beautiful girls are the loneliest.
Person2: That's a crock of shit.
Person1: I know.

Person1: Or what if there really was an error? -- I don't know what kind -- any kind that needed correcting -- and what if Burgel was responsible for it?  One mistake -- even a small one in a firm like ours -- it could cost him a promotion.
Person2: First these nameless authorities were the root of all evil, now it's insignificant Burgel.  If indeed there was a mistake -- and a minor one at that -- you're suggesting someone went to a lot of trouble over something so trivial as to not matter at all.
Person1: What seems important to these people is not determined by the amount of work it entails -- you're far from understanding the authorities if you believe that.
Person2: Now Burgel's one of the authorities?

Person1: That's what I'm trying to... to... okay, nevermind.  Just lemme talk to Joey to... to apologize.
Person2: He's in bed!  You're not gonna wake him and make him crazy, do you understand?  He comes home from the zoo, he wants to know if Elliot's a "war hero" like you... he wants to know how many people you killed...
Person1: "Elliot"?  The heroic goddamn fireman?
Person2: I had to explain your tendancy to "exaggerate", How you were actually "in country" all of two weeks and how you killed about as many people as the other clerk-typists in your outfit, no more, no less...
Person1: Three weeks, Ev.  I didn't tell him I killed anybody...
Person2: Maybe not,... but you let him believe it!  And then I gotta explain about the homeless...
Person1: The homeless!
Person2: How not all of them own apartment complexes, how not all of them play the stock market, how not all of them rent babies when they're panhandling.  He's ten years old, Bernie!  Impressionable!

Person1: You see, Rose? Now that's the kind of dress you ought to wear.
Person2: Yeah, it's kinda nice.
Person1: You look very pretty in it, Rose. Really, you do.
Person2: Well, you're real sweet to make it for me.  There never was nobody like you. And you don't need to worry, I ain't goin' out no more.
Person1: Well, there's no reason you shouldn't go out, Rose... in moderation.
Person2: I don't want to. When I was lyin' there in that hospital bed at death's door I says to myself, I ain't goin' out no more. If I ever get outta this, I'll just stay home.

Person1: --and so determined to live that I'm going to get out and take a taxi if you don't watch where you're going.
Person2: I suppose this is going to sound absurd. I've only known you twenty-four hours, but I'm in love with you.
Person1: It doesn't usually take that long, but I'll forgive you -- you were in a tough spot when I met you.
Person2: No, really -- I'm crazy about you.
Person1: Oh, is that what caused it?
Person2: You don't believe me. You think I'm just talking. Will you marry me?
Person1: How much money have you got?
Person2: Heaven knows. A lot.
Person1: Well, that being the case, I'll take it up with the board of directors. Hey, listen, aviator, here we are!

Person1: Fucking.  She says fucking in front of an entire room of people.
Person2: I said I was sorry.
Person1: Did you hear it?

Person1: That's the whole point, Jeffrey. That's the whole point. Anyone's. Everyone's. They are gonna look under every rock, dig up every flaw, every mistake you've ever made. They are going to distort and exaggerate everything you've ever done, man. Don't you understand?
Person2: What does this have to do with my testimony?
Person1: That's not the point.
Person2: What does this have to do with my testimony?! I told the truth! It's valid and true and provable!
Person1: That's not the point, whether you told the truth or not! Hello...?
Person2: I told the truth... I told the truth.

Person1: I've analyzed the data, and I think we should switch our marketing strategy to target a younger demographic.
Person2: Oh, Lucas, always with a new theory. You think you’re so smart.
Person1: It's not about thinking I'm smart, Nina. It's about adapting to the market.
Person2: Maybe we should get more opinions before making any drastic changes.
Person1: That's fair. Let's discuss it in the next team meeting.

Person1: I mean, what is there to be happy about really? I mean, going to the gym, climbing the StairMaster, eating the yogurt, checking the voice mail. Smoking the low-tar cigarettes, shaving the bikini line. Sometimes I just want something different, you know?
Person2: What was your name again?
Person1: Erica.
Person2: Erica. So do you think you and I are alike, Erica?
Person1: Deep down. Way down.
Person2: It's a mistake to think that.
Person1: We could still talk, you know? It's nice to talk.
Person2: It's nice to do a lot of things.
Person1: That's what I mean.
Person2: I'm not a nice guy.
Person1: I know. It's okay.
Person2: Yeah?
Person1: Mm-hm. I'm a big girl. I can take care of myself. If I didn't want to be here, I'd be in a limo right now with a bunch of kids looking for Chinese food.
Person2: No, no, see, you, you don't understand.
Person1: Hay? Yeah? So teach me a lesson.

Person1: After you change, if you're not busy elsewhere, we can have supper.
Person2: I'd love to! Or should I pretend I'm busy?
Person1: Let's have a minimum of pretending. I'll want to do a column about you-
Person2: I'm not enough for a paragraph.
Person1: - perhaps more than one. There's so much I want to know. I've heard your story in bits and pieces... your home in Wisconsin, your tragic marriage, your financial attachment to Margo - it started in San Francisco, didn't it?  I say - your idolatry of Margo started in San Francisco, didn't it?
Person2: That's right.
Person1: San Francisco. An oasis of civilization in the California desert. Tell me, do you share my high opinion of San Francisco?
Person2: Yes. I do.
Person1: And that memorable night when Margo first dazzled you from the stage - which theater was it in San Francisco? Was it - the Shubert?
Person2: Yes. The Shubert.
Person1: A fine old theater, the Shubert. Full of tradition, untouched by the earthquake - so sorry - fire... by the way, what was your husband's name?
Person2: Eddie...
Person1: Eddie what?

Person1: I'm sorry to do this to ya here in front of a crowd, but I want ya to stand up and make a nice apology to my girl.
Person2: I'm sorry.

Person1: How could you do this to me tonight? I told you I had company
Person2: Sorry. It's important.

Person1: Well sure, look at it!  Young trophy wife, I mean, in the parlance of our times, owes money all over town, including to known pornographers-- and that's cool, that's cool-- but I'm saying, she needs money, and of course they're gonna say they didn't get it 'cause she wants more, man, she's gotta feed the monkey, I mean-- hasn't that ever occurred to you...? Sir?
Person2: No.  No Mr. Lebowski, that had not occurred to me.

Person1: Directing suits you.
Person2: I'm not so sure.  Look again in three months.
Person1: It must be nice having so many strangers kiss your ass all of a sudden.
Person2: Ow!  Gina, you obviously need to relax.
Person1: Just did.  Jealous?
Person2: When does he graduate high school?
Person1: Oh, very jealous.

Person1: There's a slot for a boss loader on the new pier we're opening up.
Person2: Boss loader!
Person1: Ten cents a hundred pounds on everything that moves in and out. And you don't have to lift a finger. It'll be three-four hundred a week just for openers.
Person2: And for all that dough I don't do nothin'?
Person1: Absolutely nothing. You do nothing and you say nothing. You understand, don't you, kid?
Person2: Yeah yeah I guess I do but there's a lot more to this whole thing than I thought, Charley.
Person1: You don't mean you're thinking of testifying against
Person2: I don't know I don't know! I tell you I ain't made up my mind yet. That's what I wanted to talk to you about.
Person1: Listen, Terry, these piers we handle through the locals you know what they're worth to us?
Person2: I know. I know.
Person1: Well, then, you know Cousin Johnny isn't going to jeopardize a setup like that for one rubber-lipped
Person2: Don't say that!
Person1: ex-tanker who's walking on his heels ?
Person2: Don't say that!
Person1: What the hell!!!
Person2: I could have been better!
Person1: The point is there isn't much time, kid.

Person1: Go home.
Person2: May I see my mother?
Person1: You do not want to be here.  Go home to your father.
Person2: She did not do it... please listen...
Person1: She is guilty.
Person2: No, sir, please... she is innocent...
Person1: She has confessed.
Person2: No.
Person1: She has admitted to her evil and signed a written confession.  Do you know what confession is?  All that is left for you now is to pray for her soul.

Person1: Then what do you do?
Person2: I sleep it off.
Person1: But between the time you leave the cant ma and fall into bed in that old truck of yours, what happens, Charlie?
Person2: I don't know.
Person1: That's just it, Charlie. That's what I'm driving at. You could do anything in that time.

Person1: Your family history is very important to you, isn't it?
Person2: Ever since I was a little boy, I remember hearing about the family line. The Picards that fought at Trafalgar, the Picards that settled the first Martian colony. When my brother married and had a son...

Person1: You had them eating out of your hand, kiddo.
Person2: They had their minds made up before I even went in there. They think I'm a head case.
Person1: You are a head case.  Have a donut.

Person1: Will, our offer starts you at eighty- four thousand a year, plus benefits.
Person2: Retainer...
Person1: You want us to give you cash right now?
Person2: Allegedly, what I am saying is your situation will be concurrently improved if I had two hundred sheets in my pocket right now.

Person1: Do you know what he's saying?
Person2: What who's saying?
Person1: Manzanita....  ... if one day I die, and you read this piece of paper, I want you to know how much I love you. Although I'll never see you again, Gypsy, Gypsy, your hair, your hair, your face, your face'

Person1: He's getting up.
Person2: You can't leave me then, can you?

Person1: I have this thing where I have to know a person is being honest with me before, you know, I can feel completely free to be myself.
Person2: We're strangers in the night. The last thing we want is honesty.
Person1: What do we want?
Person2: Mystery. Deception.
Person1: Deception isn't something I personally consider sexy.
Person2: What's sexy?
Person1: Knowing who a person is. Down deep.
Person2: Even if the truth about a person is sad or depressing or shocking?
Person1: You won't even tell me your name. What's shocking about a name?

Person1: And youdon't tell her.  What you're going to say.
Person2: Not use my words, please!
Person1: No, baby.  You're gonna use my words.

Person1: I can't go on like this.  I can't....  I don't just sit around and watch people.  If I make a mistake, it's all over.  You see it every day.  Do you want to see me taken away in pieces?
Person2: I don't want to lose you.

Person1: Isabella?  Are you crazy, man?  She almost got us killed.  She's setting you up again!
Person2: No.  I'm going to find out.
Person1: Wait a second.  There's guards all over the place.  You'll need a distraction.

Person1: Twenty-seven fifty-three... do you see it?  That must be it...
Person2: Great...
Person1: What?! It looks totally normal... what's wrong with it?
Person2: I said "great"...
Person1: Oh yeah, I can tell you really love it!
Person2: Well, what am I supposed to say? "I can't wait to live in some depressing shit-hole in the middle of nowhere"?!
Person1: There's something wrong with every single place we look at! Why don't you just come right out and tell me you don't want to move in with me?!
Person2: Because you'll freak out and act like a total psycho about it.

Person1: If you want an answer to that question I can always put one of my men on the job. Good afternoon, Mr. Cross.
Person2: Mr. Gittes!  You're dealing with a disturbed woman who's lost her husband. I don't want her taken advantage of. Sit down.
Person1: What for?
Person2: You may think you know what you're dealing with, but believe me, you don't.

Person1: I can't believe he put the moves on me. After everything I've done for him.
Person2: He's scum! As far as he's concerned you're just a pair of breasts that talk.

Person1: If I were you, I'd worry less about the shower and more about Rick's bachelor party.
Person2: Ilene, why would I want to do that? I trust Rick.
Person1: Of course you do. I trusted my ex, Mel, too. Cousin, I can only talk from experience. What do you think they do at these parties, have tea and play scrabble?
Person2: Ilene, Rick promised...
Person1: Debbie, don't be naive. Men are pigs.

Person1: Go on.
Person2: I like your bar.

Person2: I want your bar.

And you wouldn't know about real loss, because that only occurs when you lose something you love more than yourself, and you've never dared to love anything that much. I look at you and I don't see an intelligent confident man, I don't see a peer, and I don't see my equal. I see a boy. Nobody could possibly understand you, right Will? The firm of Bracegirdle and Chatwick, in the city of London, are prepared to lend you 20,000 pounds, pledged against your interest in the Edric mines.  They will redeem the encumbrances against the property, which amount to some 10,000 pounds, and take a twenty- year working lease on the mines. They will lend you the 20,000 pounds against the lease income, which they will apply to the loan as it comes in, and they will make a charge of 18% per annum interest on the outstanding loan balance.

Person1: Considering your financial circumstances, Mr. Cosgrove, it has been impossible to find anyone at all prepared to do any business with you.  I think you may count yourself lucky to have this opportunity. But, obviously, if you would reject this offer, I shall keep trying to find a better one.

Person1: May I be quite frank with you?
Person2: Yes, of course.
Person1: Mister Bracegirdle said to me that he had heard her ladyship lives in some fear of her life, and meditated a separation, in which case, she might later repudiate any documents signed by herself while in durance, and subject them, at any rate, to a doubtful and expensive litigation. They were quite insistent on this point, and said they must have absolute assurance of her ladyship's perfect freewill in the transaction before they would advance a shilling of their capital.
Person2: I see.
Person1: When I asked them in what form they would accept her ladyship's assurances, they said that they were only prepared to accept them if her ladyship confirms her written consent by word of mouth, in their presence, at their counting-house in Birchin Lane, London.  I requested they come here, and save her ladyship and yourself the inconvenience of the trip to London, but they declined, saying that they did not wish to incur the risk of a visit to Castle Hackton to negotiate, as they were aware of how other respectable parties, such as Messrs. Sharp and Salomon had been treated here.
